title: Deeper Questions in AI Safety
summary: A guided note for exploring the harder philosophical questions behind AI safety: what we should align AI to, whose values matter, and what it means for powerful systems to act in human interests.

notes.heading: Why philosophy enters the picture
notes.body: AI safety is not only about preventing technical failures. Once systems become powerful enough to shape the world, we also have to ask what goals, values, and constraints they should follow.

notes.heading: Alignment is not just obedience
notes.body: A system that simply follows instructions can still cause harm if the instructions are vague, manipulated, short-sighted, or based on flawed human preferences.

notes.heading: Look for unresolved tensions
notes.body: Many deep safety questions do not have clean answers yet: individual versus collective values, corrigibility versus autonomy, short-term usefulness versus long-term control, and what counts as genuinely beneficial behavior.

references.title: The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
references.note: A broad early discussion of moral, social, and existential questions around advanced AI.

references.title: Coherent Extrapolated Volition
references.note: A classic proposal for thinking about what it could mean to align AI with idealized human values.

references.title: The Off-Switch Game
references.note: Explores corrigibility: why a beneficial AI system should allow itself to be corrected or shut down.

author: Shelvia Wongso
